title: Sea Level Datum of 1929

infobox.value: |  | | --- | --- | | [NGVD 29](/wiki/Sea_Level_Datum_of_1929 "Sea Level Datum of 1929") | Sea Level Datum 1929 | | [OSGB36](/wiki/OSGB36 "OSGB36") | Ordnance Survey Great Britain 1936 | | [SK-42](/wiki/SK-42_reference_system "SK-42 reference system") | Systema Koordinat 1942 goda | | [ED50](/wiki/ED50 "ED50") | European Datum 1950 | | [SAD69](/wiki/South_American_Datum#SAD69 "South American Datum") | South American Datum 1969 | | [GRS 80](/wiki/GRS_80 "GRS 80") | Geodetic Reference System 1980 | | [ISO 6709](/wiki/ISO_6709 "ISO 6709") | Geographic point coord. 1983 | | [NAD 83](/wiki/North_American_Datum#North_American_Datum_of_1983 "North American Datum") | North American Datum 1983 | | [WGS 84](/wiki/World_Geodetic_System "World Geodetic System") | World Geodetic System 1984 | | [NAVD 88](/wiki/North_American_Vertical_Datum_of_1988 "North American Vertical Datum of 1988") | N. American Vertical Datum 1988 | | [ETRS89](/wiki/European_Terrestrial_Reference_System_1989 "European Terrestrial Reference System 1989") | European Terrestrial Ref. Sys. 1989 | | [GCJ-02](/wiki/Restrictions_on_geographic_data_in_China "Restrictions on geographic data in China") | Chinese obfuscated datum 2002 | | [Geo URI](/wiki/Geo_URI_scheme "Geo URI scheme") | Internet link to a point 2010 |  * [International Terrestrial Reference System](/wiki/International_Terrestrial_Reference_System "International Terrestrial Reference System") * [Spatial Reference System Identifier (SRID)](/wiki/SRID "SRID") * [Universal Transverse Mercator (UTM)](/wiki/Universal_Transverse_Mercator_coordinate_system "Universal Transverse Mercator coordinate system")

(**NGVD29**), originally known as the **Sea Level Datum of 1929**,[[1]](#cite_note-NG29-1) is the [vertical datum](/wiki/Vertical_datum "Vertical datum") established for vertical control [surveying](/wiki/Surveying "Surveying") in the [United States of America](/wiki/United_States "United States") by the General Adjustment of 1929. NGVD29 was determined and published by the [United States Coast and Geodetic Survey](/wiki/United_States_Coast_and_Geodetic_Survey "United States Coast and Geodetic Survey")[[2]](#cite_note-2) and is used to measure the [elevation](/wiki/Elevation "Elevation") of a point above and below [mean sea level](/wiki/Mean_sea_level "Mean sea level") (MSL).
[.esri.com GIS Dictionary: National Geodetic Vertical Datum of 1929 Accessed November 18, 2023](https://support.esri.com/en-us/gis-dictionary/national-geodetic-vertical-datum-of-1929)

US Department of Commerce, National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration. ["North American Vertical Datum of 1988 (NAVD 88) - Vertical Datum - Datums - National Geodetic Survey"](https://www.ngs.noaa.gov/datums/vertical/north-american-vertical-datum-1988.shtml). *www.ngs.noaa.gov*. Retrieved February 1, 2022.

["Standards and Procedures for Referencing Project Elevation Grades to Nationwide Vertical Datums"](https://www.publications.usace.army.mil/Portals/76/Publications/EngineerManuals/EM_1110-2-6056.pdf) (PDF).

| * [v](/wiki/Template:Geodesy "Template:Geodesy") * [t](/wiki/Template_talk:Geodesy "Template talk:Geodesy") * [e](/wiki/Special:EditPage/Template:Geodesy "Special:EditPage/Template:Geodesy") |

The **National Geodetic Vertical Datum of 1929** (**NGVD29**), originally known as the **Sea Level Datum of 1929**,[[1]](#cite_note-NG29-1) is the [vertical datum](/wiki/Vertical_datum "Vertical datum") established for vertical control [surveying](/wiki/Surveying "Surveying") in the [United States of America](/wiki/United_States "United States") by the General Adjustment of 1929. NGVD29 was determined and published by the [United States Coast and Geodetic Survey](/wiki/United_States_Coast_and_Geodetic_Survey "United States Coast and Geodetic Survey")[[2]](#cite_note-2) and is used to measure the [elevation](/wiki/Elevation "Elevation") of a point above and below [mean sea level](/wiki/Mean_sea_level "Mean sea level") (MSL).

NGVD29 was superseded by the [North American Vertical Datum of 1988](/wiki/North_American_Vertical_Datum_of_1988 "North American Vertical Datum of 1988") (NAVD88),[[3]](#cite_note-3) based upon reference to a single benchmark (referenced to the new [International Great Lakes Datum of 1985](/wiki/International_Great_Lakes_Datum_of_1985 "International Great Lakes Datum of 1985") local mean sea level height value), although many cities and [U.S. Army Corps of Engineers](/wiki/U.S._Army_Corps_of_Engineers "U.S. Army Corps of Engineers") "legacy" projects with established data continued to use the older datum.[[4]](#cite_note-4)

## Methodology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=National_Geodetic_Vertical_Datum_of_1929&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Methodology")]

Mean sea level was measured at 26 tide gauges: 21 in the United States and five in [Canada](/wiki/Canada "Canada"). The datum was defined by the observed heights of mean sea level at the 26 tide gauges and by the set of elevations of all bench marks resulting from the [adjustment of observations](/wiki/Adjustment_of_observations "Adjustment of observations"). The adjustment required a total of 66,315 miles (106,724 km) of [levelling](/wiki/Levelling "Levelling") with 246 closed circuits and 25 circuits at sea level.

Since the Sea Level Datum of 1929 was a hybrid model, it was not a pure model of [mean sea level](/wiki/Mean_sea_level "Mean sea level"), the [geoid](/wiki/Geoid "Geoid"), or any other [equipotential](/wiki/Equipotential "Equipotential") surface. Therefore, it was renamed the National Geodetic Vertical Datum of 1929 (NGVD 29) May 10, 1973, by the [National Geodetic Survey](/wiki/National_Geodetic_Survey "National Geodetic Survey"), a part of the [National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration](/wiki/National_Oceanic_and_Atmospheric_Administration "National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration").[[1]](#cite_note-NG29-1)
